title: Timeline on my Website Tour - Stoned

enrichment.summary: Kyle tours his personal history timeline website, a multi-section project spanning 15 billion years into the past to 52,000 years into the future, organized into six interlinked sections (prehistory, BC, CE, a 3000-year dense section, and future). He explains its design philosophy: five-column pages for visual and mental symmetry, 12 exponentially-scaling time dimensions, 99 color-and-font-coded event types (11 categories × 9 causes), and UN subregion navigation. He argues that a complete, condensed timeline makes it possible for anyone to become 'history literate' and predict current events by recognizing historical patterns.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: knowing the full scope and outer boundaries of a subject makes it feel smaller and easier to learn
five columns is the perfect number for lists — visually and mentally symmetrical, best use of page width
people who know history can identify patterns in current events and make better decisions
people learn better when they can skip around freely, so a complete navigable timeline beats a linear book
prehistory must scale exponentially because human knowledge of the past decreases exponentially the further back you go

claims.subject: history timeline
claims.claim: The history timeline has five different sections, with BC and CE being the biggest.

claims.subject: BC section
claims.claim: The BC section has 200 pages and covers from zero to 5000 years back.

claims.subject: CE section
claims.claim: The CE section has 160 pages and covers from zero to the present (2000 years).

claims.subject: 80 section
claims.claim: The 80 section has 2390 pages.

claims.subject: future section
claims.claim: The future section has 160 pages.

claims.subject: prehistory pages
claims.claim: The prehistory pages consist of 58 pages covering different time dimensions.

claims.subject: 3000 years back section
claims.claim: The 3000 years back section has two pages: one from present to 500 and another from 500 to 3000 years back.

claims.subject: history timeline purpose
claims.claim: The purpose of the timeline is to help users memorize all recorded historical events and categorize them for better historical literacy.

claims.subject: history literacy
claims.claim: History literacy allows people to identify types of current events and predict their outcomes.

claims.subject: complete history timeline
claims.claim: The complete history timeline includes everything that ever happened in recorded and non-recorded history, as well as the future.

claims.subject: timeline sections
claims.claim: The timeline is divided into six interlinkable sections: prehistory, BC, CE, 3000 years back, dense AMP, and future.

claims.subject: prehistory section
claims.claim: The prehistory section goes as far back as 15 billion years.

claims.subject: future section
claims.claim: The future section goes 52,000 years into the future.

claims.subject: total time span
claims.claim: The total time span covered by the timeline is 15 billion and 52,000 years.

claims.subject: historical events format
claims.claim: Historical events on the timeline are listed in a format with five columns per page.

claims.subject: five columns
claims.claim: Five columns per page are used because they provide visual and mental symmetry.

claims.subject: column width
claims.claim: The width of the columns is optimized to fit the number of words that almost take up the column width.

claims.subject: BC and CE sections
claims.claim: In the BC and CE sections, each column represents 100 years, and the width of the page represents 500 years.

claims.subject: 3000 years gap pages
claims.claim: The 3000 years gap pages condense time differently, with each column representing 500 years and the page width representing 2500 years.

claims.subject: time dimensions
claims.claim: The timeline can be divided into 12 total time dimensions.

claims.subject: future time dimensions
claims.claim: In the future sections, time dimensions increase by a factor of 10, twice, and five once.

claims.subject: prehistory time dimensions
claims.claim: In the prehistory section, time dimensions increase by factors of 5, 4, 3, and 10 (multiple times).

claims.subject: horizontal timeline section
claims.claim: At the top of each page is a traditional horizontal timeline section.

claims.subject: world division
claims.claim: Each page divides the world into 16 sections based on the 19 United Nations subregions.

claims.subject: navigation
claims.claim: The timeline is linked so users can navigate to any United Nations subregion within the current page or to any other 500-year page in the section.

claims.subject: event types
claims.claim: Events are divided into 99 types created by combining 11 event categories and 9 event causes.

claims.subject: event coding
claims.claim: Event types are color and font coded to reflect their category and cause.

claims.subject: complete knowledge
claims.claim: The idea of the timeline is to make it possible to memorize all historical events and categorize them in any needed context.

claims.subject: non-recorded history
claims.claim: Non-recorded history is included in the timeline, extending back exponentially in time.

claims.subject: learning non-linearly
claims.claim: The timeline allows users to learn history non-linearly by skipping around to different sections.

claims.subject: complete grid
claims.claim: A condensed grid format makes historical knowledge seem smaller and more manageable by showing boundaries and quantities.

claims.subject: interlinkable sections
claims.claim: The six sections of the timeline are interlinkable, allowing navigation between them.

claims.subject: prehistory navigation
claims.claim: In the prehistory section, users can navigate from 30,000 years back to 15 billion years back.

claims.subject: BC navigation
claims.claim: The BC section allows navigation through 500-year segments from 5000 BC to zero.

claims.subject: CE navigation
claims.claim: The CE section allows navigation through 500-year segments from zero to the present, with more recent years condensed further.

claims.subject: future navigation
claims.claim: The future section increases exponentially, with segments like 2000-2050, 2050-2100, and up to 52,000 years.

claims.subject: time dimensions explanation
claims.claim: Time dimensions expand like a microscope, with smaller segments nested within larger ones.

claims.subject: horizontal timeline demonstration
claims.claim: The horizontal timeline section shows events stacked vertically to compare simultaneous occurrences.

claims.subject: United Nations sections
claims.claim: The world is divided into 16 sections based on United Nations subregions, organized by cultural and geographic proximity.

claims.subject: event categories
claims.claim: Event categories include political, positive, negative, and people types, each with subcategories like country, city, building, invention, war, and leader.

claims.subject: event causes
claims.claim: Event causes are divided into practical, cultural, and theoretical, such as conflict, transportation, and natural causes.

claims.subject: event type examples
claims.claim: Mixing event categories and causes creates specific event types, like a country war (conflict + country) or a cultural event with a natural cause (new music emerging spontaneously).

claims.subject: color and font coding
claims.claim: Events are color and font coded to make scanning for specific types easier, such as inventions in green or wars in red.

claims.subject: condensed information
claims.claim: The timeline condenses vast amounts of information into small spaces, like summarizing World War I casualties in half a page of text.

claims.subject: battle details
claims.claim: Battles are detailed with numbers of participants and casualties, such as the Battle of Gallipoli with Ottoman and British forces.

claims.subject: navigation flexibility
claims.claim: Users can navigate the timeline by tracing specific countries or jumping across regions and time periods.

claims.subject: complete historical coverage
claims.claim: The timeline aims to include every significant historical event, war, king, and invention, making it a comprehensive resource.

claims.subject: visual organization
claims.claim: The timeline's visual organization, with colors, fonts, and borders, helps users quickly identify and compare events.
